title: Japanese Curry Udon

overview.description: Thick, chewy udon noodles served in a rich Japanese curry broth with tender chicken, onion and spring onions.

ingredients: 2 tbsp Japanese curry powder or mild curry powder
1 tbsp cornflour mixed with 2 tbsp cold water, optional
method: If using vacuum-packed udon, loosen the noodles according to the packet instructions.
If using frozen udon, briefly boil or steam until loosened, then drain.
Heat the oil in a large saucepan or deep frying pan.
Add the chicken and cook until lightly browned on the outside.
Remove the chicken and set aside.
Add the sliced onion to the pan.
Cook over medium heat until softened and lightly golden.
Add the garlic and ginger.
Cook for 30 seconds.
Add the flour and curry powder.
Stir and cook for 1 minute to remove the raw flour flavour and toast the spices.
Add the chicken stock gradually, stirring constantly to create a smooth sauce.
Add the soy sauce, mirin, sugar and sesame oil.
Return the chicken to the pan.
Simmer gently for 10–15 minutes, until the chicken is cooked through and the broth is richly flavoured.
If a thicker curry broth is desired, stir in the cornflour slurry gradually and simmer until thickened.
Add the udon noodles.
Simmer gently for 2–3 minutes until the noodles are hot and fully coated in the curry broth.
Taste and adjust the seasoning.
Serve topped with spring onions.

learning.did-you-know: Curry udon combines two popular Japanese foods: curry and udon noodles. Unlike Japanese curry served with rice, the curry is usually loosened into a soup-like broth designed to be eaten with noodles.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to adapt a curry into a broth, how flour and stock create body and how thick noodles interact with a strongly flavoured sauce.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A Japanese noodle dish that demonstrates how the same broad curry flavour profile can be transformed into a completely different meal by changing the consistency and pairing it with udon.
chapman-tips: The curry broth should be more fluid than a curry intended to be served with rice.
Add the flour and curry powder to the onions before adding the stock so the flour cooks properly.
Add the stock gradually to avoid lumps.
Udon absorbs liquid quickly, so keep extra hot stock or water available if the broth becomes too thick.
Don't overcook the udon; it only needs to heat through.
The chicken can be replaced with thinly sliced beef, pork or tofu.
A soft-boiled egg makes a particularly good addition.
notes.batch-cooking: Best eaten fresh. The udon absorbs the broth during storage and can become soft and swollen. If preparing ahead, store the curry broth and noodles separately.
notes.leftovers: Store the curry broth separately from the noodles where possible. Reheat the broth gently and add freshly prepared udon.
